article_title: Bun 1.1.13 Ships Memory Fixes and Jest-Style Test Sharding as Pulumi Adopts It as a First-Class Runtime

content_preview.title: Bun 1.1.13 Ships Memory Fixes and Jest-Style Test Sharding as Pulumi Adopts It as a First-Class Runtime
content_preview.summary: Anthropic's first major Bun release since acquiring the runtime tackles long-running-process leaks, adds isolate, parallel and shard test flags, and lands days after Pulumi made Bun a fully supported infrastructure-as-code runtime.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Bun, the all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, package manager, and test runner acquired by Anthropic in December 2025, released version 1.1.13 on April 21, 2026. The release targets two areas the project has been criticized for — memory management in long-running processes and gaps in its Jest-compatible test runner — and lands three days after Pulumi made Bun a fully supported runtime for its infrastructure-as-code programs.

Together, the two announcements mark an inflection po...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece at 864 words (within 400-1200 range for News). Clear section structure covering release changes, the Pulumi runtime integration, ecosystem context, and a 'What We Don't Know' section that appropriately flags unverified claims. Technical depth is appropriate for a developer-focused release note with ecosystem framing.
editor_notes.source_verification: Verified both sources via WebFetch on 2026-04-23. (1) https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/21/anthropics_bun_1113_released_with_memory_fixes — confirmed: Bun 1.1.13 released Tue 21 Apr 2026; acquired by Anthropic in December 2025; isolate/parallel/shard flags with exact descriptions matching article; zlib-ng yields up to five times faster compression; ~5 percent less memory with upgraded allocator and Libpas scavenger; Sumner quote 'reduce baseline memory usage and fix a class of hangs and crashes in long-running processes' matches verbatim; Reddit quote 'Bun is not stable enough for production nor faster than Node in production' verified; Jay V quotes on moving to Node/Electron and 'memory issues, crashes, and terrible Windows support' verified; Bun 1.0 September 2023 verified. Pulumi not mentioned in this source — the Pulumi story draws exclusively from source 2. (2) https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/04/pulumi-bun-support/ — confirmed: Apr 18, 2026 date; Pulumi 3.227.0; runtime: bun in Pulumi.yaml; 'beyond its previous role as merely a package manager option' matches the article's framing; most-requested feature since Bun 1.0; three benefits (native TypeScript, faster dependency install, npm compatibility); '4x faster startup times (5-15ms vs 60-120ms) and 6-35x faster package installs' matches verbatim; magic lambdas/callback functions unsupported due to v8 and inspector module dependence; dynamic providers unsupported; Bun 1.3 and Pulumi 3.227.0 version requirements; 'will remain open source and MIT-licensed' commitment verified.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims align with the cited sources. No hallucinated quotes detected — every direct quote (Sumner, Reddit user, Jay V, Pulumi performance numbers, Anthropic open-source commitment) appears verbatim in the corresponding source. The article correctly attributes the Pulumi-related claims exclusively to InfoQ and the Bun release details exclusively to The Register. Dates (2026-04-21 release, 2026-04-18 Pulumi announcement, Anthropic acquisition December 2025, Bun 1.0 September 2023) all verified. The 'three days after' temporal framing (Apr 21 minus Apr 18) is correct.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral throughout. The article presents both Anthropic's framing of the fixes and the contrary community criticism (Reddit user, Jay V) without editorializing. Language like 'inflection point' is used carefully and qualified with 'even as a segment of the production community continues to question its stability'. The 'What We Don't Know' section demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility — it flags that benchmarks are vendor-supplied and that Windows support was not addressed.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Bun coverage in the archive. The referenced prior article (/article/2026-03/29-openai-acquires-astral-absorbing-pythons-most-popular-developer-tools-into-its-codex-platform) exists and supports the framing of the AI-vendor-absorbs-toolchain trend. No overlap with recent developer-tools coverage (Vite 8, TypeScript 6.0, Neovim 0.12, Ruby 4.0.3 CVE, etc.) in 2026-04.
concerns: Only two sources, which is sparse. The two-source set is partially complementary (The Register covers the release, InfoQ covers the Pulumi integration) but each core narrative strand rests on a single source. The article transparently flags 'Neither article provides independent benchmarks', which partially mitigates this — but a third source (e.g., Bun's official blog, GitHub release notes, or a second outlet) would strengthen the provenance chain. Acceptable for a News-category release roundup, but noted.

Performance numbers ('up to five times faster' compression, '5 percent less memory', '4x faster startup', '6-35x faster package installs') all originate from vendors (Sumner, Pulumi). The article correctly discloses this in 'What We Don't Know', so no revision needed.
editor_notes.source_count_note: 2 sources — at the minimum for a News piece. Both are reputable trade publications (The Register, InfoQ) on the source allowlist. Flagged as sparse but sufficient given the complementary coverage split.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, well-sourced News piece. Facts, quotes, dates, and technical details all verified against both cited sources. Tone is neutral, structure is clear, and epistemic limits are disclosed transparently. Approve for publication.
